translations.literal: That summer, rebel Qiang of Liangzhou raided and shook the western provinces; the court worried.
translations.idiomatic: That summer Liangzhou Qiang rebels convulsed the west, and Luoyang fretted.

sentences.zh: 於是詔騭將左右羽林、北軍五校士及諸部兵擊之，車賀幸平樂觀餞送。

translations.literal: Thereupon an edict ordered Zhi to lead the left and right feathered forest, northern army five-camp soldiers, and troops of the various commands to strike them; the imperial carriage visited Pingleguan to send them off with a feast.
translations.idiomatic: The court ordered Deng Zhi to take the feathered forest guards, the northern army's five camps, and allied columns against them, while the emperor rode to Pingleguan to stage a farewell banquet.

sentences.zh: 騭西屯漢陽，使征西校尉任尚、從事中郎司馬鈞與羌戰，大敗。

translations.literal: Zhi encamped west at Hanyang, sent Colonel on the West Ren Shang and attendant clerk Sima Jun to fight the Qiang, and was greatly defeated.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Zhi camped west at Hanyang and detached Ren Shang and Sima Jun; their battle with the Qiang ended in disaster.

sentences.zh: 時以轉輸疲弊，百姓苦役。

translations.literal: At the time because transport was exhausted and worn, the hundred surnames suffered under corvée.
translations.idiomatic: Supply lines were broken and the people groaned under endless labor levies.

sentences.zh: 冬，征騭班師。

translations.literal: In winter Zhi was summoned to withdraw the army.
translations.idiomatic: That winter the court recalled Deng Zhi's army.

sentences.zh: 朝廷以太后故，遣五官中郎將迎拜騭為大將軍。

translations.literal: The court, because of the empress dowager, sent a general of the household for all purposes at the medium rank to welcome and appoint Zhi as grand general.
translations.idiomatic: For the empress dowager's sake the throne sent a household general of the fifth grade to escort Deng Zhi home as grand general.

sentences.zh: 軍到河南，使大鴻臚親迎，中常侍賫牛、酒郊勞，王、主以下候望於道。

translations.literal: When the army reached south of the River, it sent the grand herald to welcome in person; a regular attendant brought oxen and wine to feast them in the suburbs; kings and princesses downward waited and watched on the road.
translations.idiomatic: South of the Yellow River the grand herald met him in person, a chief eunuch brought oxen and wine for a suburban feast, and princes and princesses lined the route.

sentences.zh: 既至，大會群臣，賜束帛乘馬，寵靈顯赫，光震都鄙。

translations.literal: When he arrived, there was a great meeting of the host of ministers; he was granted bundles of silk and riding horses; favor and numinous brilliance were manifest, his glory shook capital and countryside.
translations.idiomatic: His return was marked by a full-dress audience, gifts of silk and mounts, and honors so dazzling that they lit up the capital and the suburbs.

sentences.zh: 時，遭元二之災，人士荒饑，死者相望，盜賊群起，四夷侵畔。

translations.literal: At that time they encountered the disasters of the yuan and er years; gentlemen and commoners were desolate with famine, the dead faced one another, bandits rose in swarms, and the four barbarians invaded and rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: The empire was reeling from the famines of the yuan-er years: corpses lined the roads, rebels sprang up everywhere, and border tribes raided at will.

sentences.zh: 騭等崇節儉，罷力役，推進天下賢士何熙、祋諷、羊浸、李郃、陶敦等，列於朝廷；

translations.literal: Zhi and the others honored frugality, stopped forced labor projects, and advanced worthy men of the realm—He Xi, Dui Feng, Yang Jin, Li He, Tao Dun, and others—listing them at court;
translations.idiomatic: The Deng brothers preached austerity, canceled grandiose corvée projects, and brought He Xi, Dui Feng, Yang Jin, Li He, Tao Dun, and other able men to the capital.

sentences.zh: 辟楊震、朱寵、陳禪，置之幕府，故天下復安。

translations.literal: summoned Yang Zhen, Zhu Chong, and Chen Chan, placing them in the headquarters, wherefore the realm returned to peace.
translations.idiomatic: They recruited Yang Zhen, Zhu Chong, and Chen Chan into their staff, and the realm slowly steadied.

sentences.zh: 四年，母新野君寢病，騭兄弟並上書求還侍養。

translations.literal: In the fourth year, the mother Lady Xinye lay gravely ill; Zhi's brothers together submitted memorials begging to return and attend and nurture her.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourth year their mother, Lady Xinye, fell dangerously ill; the brothers begged leave together to nurse her.

sentences.zh: 太后以閶最少，孝行尤著，特聽之，賜安車駟馬。

translations.literal: The empress dowager, because Chang was the youngest and his filial conduct was especially marked, specially listened to him, granting a secure carriage and four-horse team.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager singled out the youngest, Deng Chang, for his conspicuous filial piety, granted his request, and gave him an official carriage with a four-horse team.

sentences.zh: 及新野君薨，騭等復乞身行服，章連上，太后許之。

translations.literal: When Lady Xinye died, Zhi and the others again begged in person to observe mourning; memorials came in succession; the empress dowager permitted it.
translations.idiomatic: When Lady Xinye died they asked to resign and observe the full mourning; she approved after a flurry of memorials.

sentences.zh: 騭等既還裏第，並居冢次。

translations.literal: When Zhi and the others had returned to their village mansions, they all dwelt in the mourning huts beside the tomb.
translations.idiomatic: They retired to their estates and lived in rough shelters beside her grave.

sentences.zh: 閶至孝骨立，有聞當時。

translations.literal: Chang was utmost filial until his bones stood like frames; he had a reputation in that time.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Chang mourned until he was skin and bone; contemporaries spoke of nothing else.

sentences.zh: 及服闋，詔喻騭還輔朝政，更授前封。

translations.literal: When the mourning was removed, an edict instructed Zhi to return and assist court government, again investing the former fief.
translations.idiomatic: When mourning ended an edict summoned Deng Zhi back to government and restored his old titles.

sentences.zh: 騭等叩頭固讓，乃止，於是並奉朝請，位次在三公下，特進、侯上。

translations.literal: Zhi and the others knocked their heads and firmly declined, and it stopped; thereupon they all held court summons, their place in rank below the three excellencies, above special advancement and marquis.
translations.idiomatic: They kowtowed and refused; the court let the matter drop and kept them on summons-only status, ranked below the three dukes but above ordinary marquises and men of special advancement.

sentences.zh: 其有大議，乃詣朝堂，與公卿參謀。

translations.literal: When there was great deliberation, then they went to the court hall and consulted with the dukes and ministers.
translations.idiomatic: On weighty questions they were called to the court hall to advise with the high ministers.

sentences.zh: 元初二年，弘卒。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yuanchu, Hong died.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Hong died in Yuanchu 2.

sentences.zh: 太后服齊衰，帝絲麻，並宿幸其第。

translations.literal: The empress dowager wore qi-cui; the emperor coarse hemp; both lodged and visited his mansion.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager wore second-degree mourning, the emperor third-degree, and both kept vigil at his house.

sentences.zh: 弘少治《歐陽尚書》，授帝禁中，諸儒多歸附之。

translations.literal: Hong from youth had studied the Ouyang recension of the Documents classic, instructing the emperor inside the forbidden precincts; many of the ru attached themselves to him.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Hong had mastered the Ouyang recension of the Shangshu and tutored the young emperor in the palace; scholars flocked to his banner.

sentences.zh: 初疾病，遺言悉以常服，不得用錦衣玉匣。

translations.literal: At first when ill he left testament that all should use ordinary garments and must not use brocade robes or a jade coffin box.
translations.idiomatic: His deathbed will insisted on plain shrouds—no brocade, no jade for the inner coffin.

sentences.zh: 有司奏贈弘驃騎將軍，位特進，封西平侯。

translations.literal: The responsible officials memorialized posthumously to grant Hong general of swift cavalry, rank of special advancement, and enfeoffment as marquis of Xiping.
translations.idiomatic: The ministry proposed posthumous honors: general of swift cavalry, special advancement, and the marquisate of Xiping.

sentences.zh: 太后追思弘意，不加贈位衣服，但賜錢千萬，布萬匹，騭等復辭不受。

translations.literal: The empress dowager pondered Hong's intent, did not add posthumous rank or garments, but granted ten million cash and ten thousand bolts of cloth; Zhi and the others again declined and did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Mindful of his wishes, she skipped the grand titles and sent ten million cash and ten thousand bolts of silk instead; the brothers declined even that.

sentences.zh: 詔大鴻臚持節，即弘殯封子廣德為西平侯。

translations.literal: An edict ordered the grand herald to bear credentials, and at Hong's lying-in-state enfeoffed his son Guangde as marquis of Xiping.
translations.idiomatic: She then had the grand herald invest his son Guangde as marquis of Xiping at the bier.

sentences.zh: 將葬，有司復奏發五營輕車騎士，禮儀如霍光故事，太后皆不聽，但白蓋雙騎，門生挽送。

translations.literal: When about to bury, the responsible officials again memorialized to dispatch five-camps light chariot knights, the ritual following Huo Guang's precedent; the empress dowager all did not listen, but only white canopies and double riders, disciples pulling the cart to send him off.
translations.idiomatic: Officials asked for a Huo Guang–style escort of light cavalry from the five camps; she refused and sent him to the grave with white umbrellas, two outriders, and disciples tugging the hearse.

sentences.zh: 後以帝師之重，分西平之都鄉封廣德弟甫德為都鄉侯。

translations.literal: Later, because of the weight of being the emperor's tutor, she split the duxiang portion of the Xiping fief and enfeoffed Guangde's younger brother Fude as marquis of Duxiang.
translations.idiomatic: Later, to honor his role as imperial tutor, she carved a Duxiang district from the Xiping fief for Guangde's younger brother Fude.

sentences.zh: 四年，又封京子黃門侍郎珍為陽安侯，邑三千五百戶。

translations.literal: In the fourth year she again enfeoffed Jing's son, gentleman at the yellow gates Zhen, as marquis of Yang'an, with three thousand five hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourth year Deng Zhen, son of Deng Jing and a gentleman at the yellow gates, was made marquis of Yang'an with thirty-five hundred households.

sentences.zh: 五年，悝、閶相繼並卒，皆遺言薄葬，不受爵贈，太后並從之。

translations.literal: In the fifth year Kui and Chang died one after the other; both left instructions for simple burials and not to accept rank or posthumous gifts; the empress dowager followed all of it.
translations.idiomatic: The next year Deng Kui and Deng Chang died in quick succession, each asking for a humble funeral and no posthumous titles; she granted every wish.

sentences.zh: 乃封悝子廣宗為葉侯，閶子忠為西華侯。

translations.literal: Thereupon she enfeoffed Kui's son Guangzong as marquis of Ye and Chang's son Zhong as marquis of Xihua.
translations.idiomatic: She then named Guangzong as the marquis of Ye and Zhong as the marquis of Xihua.

sentences.zh: 自祖父禹教訓子孫，皆遵法度，深戒竇氏，檢敕宗族，闔門靜居。

translations.literal: From the time of grandfather Yu instructing sons and grandsons, all obeyed the statutes, deeply warned against the Dou clan, restrained the kin group, and kept the gates closed in quiet living.
translations.idiomatic: Since Deng Yu's day the family had lived by the code, shunned Dou-style excess, policed their own kin, and kept a low profile behind closed doors.

sentences.zh: 騭子侍中鳳，嘗與尚書郎張龕書，屬郎中馬融宜在臺閣。

translations.literal: Zhi's son, attendant Feng, once wrote to gentleman of the masters Zhang Kan, entrusting that gentleman Ma Rong was suitable for terrace and pavilion posts.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Feng, Deng Zhi's son and a palace attendant, once wrote Zhang Kan of the masters' office asking that Ma Rong be considered for a secretariat post.

sentences.zh: 又中郎將任尚嘗遺鳳馬，後尚坐斷盜軍糧，檻車征詣廷尉，鳳懼事泄，先自首於騭。

translations.literal: Also Colonel Ren Shang once presented Feng with horses; later Shang sat guilt for cutting off and embezzling army grain, was caged in a cart and summoned to the commandant of justice; Feng feared the affair would leak and first self-reported to Zhi.
translations.idiomatic: Colonel Ren Shang had once given Deng Feng horses; when Ren was arrested for stealing the army grain, Feng feared exposure and confessed to his father first.

sentences.zh: 騭畏太后，遂髡妻及鳳以謝，天下稱之。

translations.literal: Zhi feared the empress dowager, thereupon shaved his wife and Feng to apologize; the realm praised him.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Zhi, terrified of the empress dowager's wrath, had his wife and son shaved as criminals to atone; the empire called it righteous.

sentences.zh: 大司農朱寵痛騭無罪遇禍，乃肉輿櫬，上疏追訟騭曰：『伏惟和熹皇后聖善之德，為漢文母。

translations.literal: The minister of agriculture Zhu Chong grieved that Zhi met calamity without crime; thereupon in flesh he carried a coffin and submitted a memorial suing for Zhi, saying: 'I humbly consider that Empress Hexi, in holy and good virtue, is for Han a pattern-mother.'
translations.idiomatic: Minister of Agriculture Zhu Chong, convinced Deng Zhi was innocent, shouldered a coffin bare-chested and memorialized: 'Empress Hexi's sagely goodness makes her the mother-pattern of the Han house.'

sentences.zh: 兄弟忠孝，同心憂國，宗廟有主，王室是賴。

translations.literal: The brothers were loyal and filial, of one mind grieving for the state; the ancestral temple had its master; the royal house relied on them.
translations.idiomatic: Her brothers served with loyal hearts; the altars had a pillar, and the Liu house leaned on them.

sentences.zh: 功成身退，讓國遜位，歷世外戚，無與為比。

translations.literal: Merit accomplished, they retired their persons, yielding state and yielding rank; through the generations of outer kin there was none with whom to compare them.
translations.idiomatic: They won the peace, then stepped back and yielded power—no other consort clan in history matched their restraint.

sentences.zh: 當享積善履謙之祐，而橫為宮人單辭所陷。

translations.literal: They ought to have enjoyed the blessing of accumulated good and practiced modesty, yet they were violently trapped by a palace woman's single-sided words.
translations.idiomatic: They deserved the reward of virtue and humility; instead a palace woman's one-sided tale ruined them.

sentences.zh: 利口傾險，反亂國家，罪無申證。

translations.literal: Sharp mouths are treacherous; they overturn and disorder the state; their crimes lack corroborating proof.
translations.idiomatic: Slanderous tongues poison the state, yet no evidence backed the charge.

sentences.zh: 獄不訊鞠，遂令騭等罹此酷濫。

translations.literal: The prison did not examine and question, and thereupon caused Zhi and the others to fall into this cruel excess.
translations.idiomatic: There was no real trial, yet Deng Zhi and his kin were crushed in a judicial atrocity.

sentences.zh: 一門七人，並不以命，屍骸流離，怨魂不反，逆天感人，率土喪氣。

translations.literal: One gate, seven persons, none died a natural fate; corpses drifted apart; resentful souls did not return; they opposed Heaven and moved men; the whole realm lost its breath.
translations.idiomatic: Seven members of one family were cut down unnaturally; the bodies lay unclaimed; angry ghosts, they say, could not find rest; all under Heaven went numb with the grief.

sentences.zh: 宜收還冢次，寵樹遺孤，奉承血祀，以謝亡靈。

translations.literal: It is fitting to gather and return them to the tomb site, favor and raise the orphaned survivors, receive and perform the blood sacrifice, thereby to answer the departed spirits.
translations.idiomatic: They should be gathered home to the family tombs, their orphans sheltered, and the ancestral rites kept so the dead may be appeased.

sentences.zh: 』寵知其言切，自致廷尉，詔免官歸田里。

translations.literal: Chong knew his words were cutting; he delivered himself to the commandant of justice; an edict dismissed him from office to return to his village fields.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Chong knew he had spoken dangerously and surrendered himself; the emperor stripped him of office and sent him home.

sentences.zh: 眾庶多為騭稱枉，帝意頗悟，乃譴讓州郡，還葬洛陽北芒舊塋，公卿皆會喪，莫不悲傷之。

translations.literal: The multitude for the most part declared Zhi wronged; the emperor's intent somewhat awakened, and he reproached the commanderies and prefectures, returning burial to the old tombs north of Mang at Luoyang; dukes and ministers all attended the funeral—none were not grieved for them.
translations.idiomatic: Popular outcry called Deng Zhi innocent; the emperor relented, rebuked local officials, and had the dead reinterred on Mang Hill. The whole court turned out, weeping.

sentences.zh: 詔遣使者祠以中牢，諸從昆弟皆歸京師。

translations.literal: An edict dispatched envoys to sacrifice with the middle ox-and-sheep victim; all cousins returned to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: He sent envoys with a mid-grade sacrifice and summoned every collateral branch back to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 及順帝即位，追感太后恩訓，湣騭無辜，乃詔宗正復故大將軍鄧騭宗親內外，朝見皆如故事。

translations.literal: When Emperor Shun ascended, he pursued gratitude for the empress dowager's gracious instruction, pitied Zhi as guiltless, and thereupon ordered the director of the imperial clan to restore the former grand general Deng Zhi's kin inside and outside, audience at court all as in former precedent.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Shun, remembering Empress Deng's care for him, cleared Deng Zhi's name and ordered the imperial clan office to restore all Deng kin to their old court privileges.

sentences.zh: 除騭兄弟子及門從十二人悉為郎中，擢朱寵為太尉，錄尚書事。

translations.literal: He removed the guilt of Zhi's brothers' sons and gate followers twelve persons, all appointing them gentlemen of the palace; he promoted Zhu Chong to grand commandant and recorder of the masters' affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Twelve of the Deng nephews and clients were cleared and made gentlemen of the palace; Zhu Chong was raised to grand commandant with control of the secretariat.

sentences.zh: 寵字仲威，京兆人，初辟騭府，稍遷潁川太守，治理有聲。

translations.literal: Chong, courtesy name Zhongwei, was a man of Jingzhao; at first he was summoned to Zhi's headquarters, gradually promoted grand administrator of Yingchuan, his governing and ordering had a reputation.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Chong of Jingzhao, courtesy name Zhongwei, had begun in Deng Zhi's staff, rose to grand administrator of Yingchuan, and earned fame as an administrator.

sentences.zh: 及拜太尉，封安鄉侯，甚加優禮。

translations.literal: When appointed grand commandant he was enfeoffed as marquis of Anxiang, and very great courteous treatment was added.
translations.idiomatic: As grand commandant he received the marquisate of Anxiang and extraordinary marks of favor.

sentences.zh: 廣德早卒。

translations.literal: Guangde died early.
translations.idiomatic: Guangde died young.

sentences.zh: 甫德更召徵為開封令。

translations.literal: Fude was again summoned and appointed magistrate of Kaifeng.
translations.idiomatic: Fude was later recalled and named magistrate of Kaifeng.

sentences.zh: 學傳父業。

translations.literal: He studied and transmitted his father's learning.
translations.idiomatic: He carried on his father's scholarship.

sentences.zh: 喪母，遂不仕。

translations.literal: When his mother died, thereupon he did not take office.
translations.idiomatic: After his mother's death he refused further office.

sentences.zh: 閶妻耿氏有節操，痛鄧氏誅廢，子忠早卒，乃養河南尹豹子，嗣後閶後。

translations.literal: Chang's wife Lady Geng had integrity of conduct; she grieved that the Deng clan was executed and abolished, and that her son Zhong had died early; thereupon she raised the son of the grand administrator of Henan, Bao, to continue Chang's line.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Chang's widow, Lady Geng, mourned the clan's ruin and her son's early death; she adopted the heir of Henan grand administrator Bao to continue Deng Chang's line.

sentences.zh: 耿氏教之書學，遂以通博稱。

translations.literal: Lady Geng taught him writing and learning, and thereupon he was praised for breadth of learning.
translations.idiomatic: She schooled the boy in letters until he was famed as a polymath.

sentences.zh: 永壽中，與伏無忌、延篤著書東觀，官至屯騎校尉。

translations.literal: In the Yongshou period, together with Fu Wuji and Yan Du he wrote books in the Eastern View; his office reached colonel of garrison cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: In the Yongshou era he joined Fu Wuji and Yan Du as a compiler in the Eastern Institute and rose to colonel of garrison cavalry.

sentences.zh: 禹曾孫香之女為桓帝後，帝又紹封度遼將軍遵子萬世為南鄉侯，拜河南尹。

translations.literal: The daughter of Yu's great-grandson Xiang was Empress Huan's empress; the emperor again continued the fief and enfeoffed Zun's son Wanshi, general who crosses the Liao, as marquis of Nanxiang, appointing him grand administrator of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: A great-granddaughter of Deng Yu became Emperor Huan's empress; the throne revived the Nanxiang marquisate for Zun's son Wanshi, general who crosses the Liao, and named him grand administrator of Henan.

sentences.zh: 及後廢，萬世下獄死，其餘宗親皆復歸故郡。

translations.literal: When the empress was deposed, Wanshi was cast into prison and died; the rest of the kin all returned to their old commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: When she was cast down, Wanshi died in prison and the other Dengs were sent back to their home commanderies.

sentences.zh: 鄧氏自中興後，累世寵貴，凡侯者二十九人，公二人，大將軍以下十三人，中二千石十四人，列校二十二人，州牧、郡守四十八人，其餘侍中、將、大夫、郎、謁者不可勝數，東京莫與為比。

translations.literal: From the Deng clan after the restoration, generation after generation enjoyed favor and nobility: in all twenty-nine who were marquises, two who were dukes, thirteen who were grand general and below, fourteen who were fully two thousand shi, twenty-two who were colonels of the columns, forty-eight who were regional shepherds and grand administrators; the rest who were palace attendants, generals, grandees, gentlemen, and ushers cannot be counted; none in the eastern capital could compare with them.
translations.idiomatic: After the Han revival the Dengs dominated honors for generations: twenty-nine marquises, two dukes, thirteen men at grand-general rank or higher, fourteen at the top salary grade, twenty-two column colonels, forty-eight governors—plus untold attendants and middle officials. No Luoyang clan equaled them.

sentences.zh: 論曰：漢世外戚，自東、西京十有餘族，非徒豪橫盈極，自取災故，必於貽釁後主，以至顛敗者，其數有可言焉。

translations.literal: The discussion says: Han consort kin from the eastern and western capitals numbered more than ten houses; it was not only that arrogance and transgression filled the limit and they themselves fetched disaster, but that they were sure to leave trouble for the later ruler until overturn and defeat—the number of such cases can be spoken of.
translations.idiomatic: The historian reflects: Eastern and Western Han produced a dozen consort clans. Many fell not merely because their pride courted ruin, but because they bequeathed poison to the next reign—too many to list.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why is it so?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 恩非己結，而權已先之；

translations.literal: The favor was not tied by themselves, yet power already preceded them;
translations.idiomatic: They had not earned the sovereign's love, yet power was already theirs.

sentences.zh: 情疏禮重，而枉性圖之；

translations.literal: affection was distant while ritual was heavy, yet they bent their natures to pursue it;
translations.idiomatic: Court etiquette treated them like kin while true intimacy was thin, and they bent themselves to seize more.

sentences.zh: 來寵方授，地既害之；

translations.literal: new favor was just granted, yet the ground already harmed them;
translations.idiomatic: Fresh favor from the throne only sharpened others' envy.

sentences.zh: 隙開勢謝，讒亦勝之。

translations.literal: the crack opened and momentum declined, then slander also overcame them.
translations.idiomatic: Once their patronage ebbed, slander finished the work.

sentences.zh: 悲哉！

translations.literal: How sad!
translations.idiomatic: A bitter sight!

sentences.zh: 騭、悝兄弟，季遠時柄，忠勞王室，而終莫之免，斯樂生所以泣而辭燕也！

translations.literal: Zhi and Kui's brothers, late grasping the handle of the age, loyally toiled for the royal house, yet in the end could not avoid it—this is why Music-master Yi wept and took leave of Yan!
translations.idiomatic: Deng Zhi and Deng Kui held power too late to save the house they served faithfully—small wonder General Yue Yi wept when he quit the state of Yan!

sentences.zh: 寇恂字子翼，上谷昌平人也，世為著姓。

translations.literal: Kou Xun, courtesy name Ziyi, was a man of Changping in Shanggu; for generations they were a noted surname.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun, courtesy name Ziyi, came from Changping in Shanggu, a family long prominent in the region.

sentences.zh: 恂初為郡功曹，太守耿況甚重之。

translations.literal: Xun at first was merit clerk of the commandery; Grand Administrator Geng Kuang greatly esteemed him.
translations.idiomatic: He began as merit clerk to the commandery; Grand Administrator Geng Kuang thought the world of him.

sentences.zh: 王莽敗，更始立。

translations.literal: When Wang Mang fell, Gengshi was established.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang fell and Liu Xuan took the throne as Emperor Gengshi.

sentences.zh: 使使者徇郡國，曰『先降者復爵位』。

translations.literal: He sent envoys to announce commanderies and kingdoms, saying 'those who first surrender will have rank and fief restored.'
translations.idiomatic: Imperial envoys rode to every commandery with a promise: the first to submit would recover their old titles.

sentences.zh: 恂從耿況迎使者於界上，況上印綬，使者納之，一宿無還意。

translations.literal: Xun followed Geng Kuang to welcome the envoy at the border; Kuang presented up the seal and ribbon; the envoy received them, one night had no intention of returning them.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun accompanied Geng Kuang to meet Gengshi's envoy at the frontier. Kuang handed over his seal of office; the envoy took it and showed no sign of giving it back the next morning.

sentences.zh: 恂勒兵入見使者，就請之。

translations.literal: Xun led troops to enter and see the envoy, approaching to request them.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun marched in, confronted the envoy, and demanded the seals.

sentences.zh: 使者不與，曰：『天王使者，功曹欲脅之邪？

translations.literal: The envoy would not give, saying: 'The Son of Heaven's envoy—does the merit clerk wish to coerce him?'
translations.idiomatic: The envoy refused. 'I speak for the Son of Heaven,' he snapped. 'Will a mere merit clerk bully me?'

sentences.zh: 』恂曰：『非敢脅使君，竊傷計之不詳也。

translations.literal: Xun said: 'I do not dare coerce the commissioner; I privately grieve that the plan was not thorough.'
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun answered, 'I would not dream of coercing you, sir—I only regret that no one thought this through.'

sentences.zh: 今天下初定，國信未宣，使君建節銜命，以臨四方，郡國莫不延頸傾耳，望風歸命。

translations.literal: Now the realm is first settled, imperial trustworthiness has not yet been proclaimed; the commissioner bears the baton and carries the mandate, coming to oversee the four quarters—commanderies and kingdoms none do not crane their necks and incline their ears, looking to the wind to submit to the mandate.
translations.idiomatic: The empire has barely been pacified and the court's word still means little. You ride here with the imperial baton: every commandery watches, straining to hear what the throne intends.

sentences.zh: 今始至上谷而先墮大信，沮向化之心，生離畔之隙，將復何以號令它郡乎？

translations.literal: Now you have only reached Shanggu and first destroy great trust, dam the hearts that incline to transformation, produce a crack of separation and rebellion—how again will you issue orders to other commanderies?'
translations.idiomatic: Yet your first stop is Shanggu, and your first act is to break faith. You will kill every hope of allegiance and seed revolt. How will you command the rest of the north after this?'

sentences.zh: 且耿府君在上谷，久為使人所親，今易之，得賢則造次未安，不緊則只更生亂。

translations.literal: Moreover Lord Geng in Shanggu has long been intimate with the people as an envoy; now to replace him—if one obtains a worthy then hastily there is no peace; if not tight then only again produces chaos.
translations.idiomatic: Geng Kuang has governed Shanggu for years and won the people's trust. Replace him with a good man and you still unsettle the district overnight; replace him with a mediocrity and you invite worse turmoil.

sentences.zh: 為使君計，莫若復之以安百姓。

translations.literal: For the commissioner's plan, nothing is better than restoring him to settle the hundred surnames.
translations.idiomatic: For your own sake, sir, give him his post back and calm the people.

sentences.zh: 』使者不應，恂左右以使者命召況。

translations.literal: The envoy did not respond; Xun's attendants on the envoy's order summoned Kuang.
translations.idiomatic: The envoy sulked in silence. Kou Xun's men, citing the envoy's authority, sent for Geng Kuang.

sentences.zh: 況至，恂進取印綬帶況。

translations.literal: When Kuang arrived, Xun advanced, took the seal and ribbon, and tied them on Kuang.
translations.idiomatic: When Kuang arrived, Kou Xun stepped forward, snatched the seal cord, and fastened it around Kuang's waist himself.

sentences.zh: 使者不得已，乃承制詔之，況受而歸。

translations.literal: The envoy had no choice, thereupon acting on imperial authority edicted him; Kuang received and returned.
translations.idiomatic: Cornered, the envoy issued a retroactive appointment under delegated authority; Kuang took office and rode home vindicated.

sentences.zh: 及王郎起，遣將徇上谷，急況發兵。

translations.literal: When Wang Lang rose, he sent a general to announce Shanggu and urgently pressed Kuang to dispatch troops.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Lang rebelled, he sent an officer to Shanggu to demand troops from Kuang at once.

sentences.zh: 恂與門下掾閔業共說況曰：『邯鄲拔起，難可信向。

translations.literal: Xun together with retainers Min Ye jointly persuaded Kuang, saying: 'Handan sprang up—hard to trust and turn to.'
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun and his clerk Min Ye urged Kuang, 'Wang Lang's regime at Handan is a sudden upstart—too risky to follow.'

sentences.zh: 昔王莽時，所難獨有劉伯升耳。

translations.literal: In the past in Wang Mang's time, the one who was difficult alone was Liu Bosheng.
translations.idiomatic: Under Wang Mang only Liu Yan—Bosheng—stood in his way.

sentences.zh: 今聞大司馬劉公，伯升母弟，尊賢下士，士多歸之，可攀附也。

translations.literal: Now we hear the grand marshal Lord Liu, Bosheng's younger brother by the same mother, honors the worthy and humbles himself before scholars; many gentlemen attach themselves to him—one can climb and attach.
translations.idiomatic: His younger brother, Grand Marshal Liu Xiu, honors talent and treats scholars with deference; able men flock to him. That is the side to join.

sentences.zh: 』況曰：『邯鄲方盛，力不能獨拒，如何？

translations.literal: Kuang said: 'Handan is just flourishing; our strength cannot alone resist—how?'
translations.idiomatic: Kuang objected, 'Wang Lang is at the height of his power. We cannot stand alone—what then?'

sentences.zh: 』恂對曰：『今上谷完實，控弦萬騎，舉大郡之資，可以詳擇去就。

translations.literal: Xun replied, saying: 'Now Shanggu is intact and full, bowmen number ten thousand horse; raising the resources of a great commandery, one can carefully choose to go or stay.'
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun replied, 'Shanggu is rich and intact, with ten thousand archers in the saddle. With the wealth of a whole commandery behind you, you can pick your moment to commit.'

sentences.zh: 恂請東約漁陽，齊心合眾，邯鄲不足圖也。

translations.literal: Xun requests to go east and treaty with Yuyang, unite hearts and combine hosts—Handan is not worth planning.
translations.idiomatic: Let me ride east and ally Yuyang. Combine our two hosts and Wang Lang is hardly worth worrying about.

sentences.zh: 』況然之，乃遣恂到漁陽，結謀彭寵。

translations.literal: Kuang approved it, thereupon sent Xun to Yuyang to plot with Peng Chong.
translations.idiomatic: Kuang agreed and sent Kou Xun to Yuyang to win over Peng Chong.

sentences.zh: 恂還，至昌平，襲擊邯鄲使者，殺之，奪其軍，遂與況子弇等俱南及光武於廣阿。

translations.literal: Xun returned, reached Changping, ambushed and struck the Handan envoy, killed him, seized his army, thereupon together with Kuang's son Yan and others all went south and reached Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: On the way back Kou Xun ambushed Wang Lang's courier at Changping, killed him, seized his column, and rode south with Geng Yan and the rest to join Liu Xiu at Guang'a.

sentences.zh: 拜恂為偏將軍，號承義侯，從破群賊。

translations.literal: He appointed Xun lieutenant general, styled marquis who receives righteousness, followed in breaking the host of bandits.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiu named him lieutenant general and marquis who receives righteousness, and he followed in the campaigns that broke the rebel hosts.

sentences.zh: 數與鄧禹謀議，禹奇之，因奉牛、酒共交歡。

translations.literal: Several times he plotted and deliberated with Deng Yu; Yu marveled at him, thereupon presenting oxen and wine they jointly exchanged good cheer.
translations.idiomatic: He conferred repeatedly with Deng Yu, who came to admire him; they sealed their friendship with oxen and wine.

sentences.zh: 光武南定河內，而更始大司馬朱鮪等盛兵據洛陽，及并州未定，光武難其守，問於鄧禹曰：『諸將誰可使守河內者？

translations.literal: Guangwu south settled Henei, but Gengshi's grand marshal Zhu You and others with strong troops held Luoyang, and Bingzhou was not yet settled; Guangwu found guarding it difficult and asked Deng Yu, saying: 'Among the generals who can be sent to guard Henei?'
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiu had secured Henei to the south, yet Zhu You and Gengshi's other marshals still held Luoyang in strength and Bingzhou remained unsettled. Unsure whom to trust with the rear, he asked Deng Yu, 'Which of my generals can hold Henei?'

sentences.zh: 』禹曰：『昔高祖任蕭何於關中，無復四顧之憂，所以得專精山東，終成大業。

translations.literal: Yu said: 'In the past the High Ancestor employed Xiao He in Guanzhong and had no further four-directions worry, wherefore he could concentrate essence east of the mountains and finally complete the great enterprise.'
translations.idiomatic: Deng Yu answered, 'Gaozu gave Guanzhong to Xiao He and never looked over his shoulder—only then could he throw everything into the east and win the empire.'

sentences.zh: 今河內帶河為固，戶口殷實，北通上黨，南迫洛陽。

translations.literal: Now Henei is girded by the River as firm, households and mouths are abundant and solid, north connects to Shangdang, south presses on Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: Henei is shielded by the Yellow River, thick with people, linked north to Shangdang and south to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 寇恂文武備足，有牧人禦眾之才，非此子莫可使也。

translations.literal: Kou Xun in civil and military is fully sufficient, has the talent to shepherd people and control the multitude—other than this youth none can be sent.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun is equal to both civil and military duty; he can govern men and lead armies. No one else will do.

sentences.zh: 』乃拜恂河內太守，行大將軍事。

translations.literal: Thereupon he appointed Xun grand administrator of Henei, acting grand general affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiu therefore made Kou Xun grand administrator of Henei with the powers of a commanding general.

sentences.zh: 光武謂恂曰：『河內完富，吾將因是而起。

translations.literal: Guangwu said to Xun: 'Henei is intact and rich; I will rely on this to rise.'
translations.idiomatic: He told Kou Xun, 'Henei is whole and prosperous—you will be the base from which I rise.'

sentences.zh: 昔高祖留蕭何鎮關中，吾今委公以河內，堅守轉運，給足軍糧，率厲士馬，防遏它兵，勿令北度而已。

translations.literal: In the past the High Ancestor left Xiao He to guard Guanzhong; I now entrust you with Henei, firmly guard and transport, supply sufficient army grain, lead and encourage soldiers and horses, block other troops, do not let them cross north—that is all.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu left Xiao He to hold Guanzhong; I leave Henei to you. Keep the supply lines open, feed my army, stiffen the troops, and block any enemy from crossing the river—that is all I ask.

sentences.zh: 』光武於是復北征燕、代。

translations.literal: Guangwu thereupon again north campaigned to Yan and Dai.
translations.idiomatic: With that, Liu Xiu marched north again against Yan and Dai.

sentences.zh: 恂移書屬縣，講兵肄射，伐淇園之竹，為矢百餘萬，養馬二千匹，收租四百萬斛，轉以給軍。

translations.literal: Xun sent letters to the subordinate counties, lectured troops and practiced archery, cut bamboo in the Qi park, made more than a million arrows, raised two thousand horses, collected rent of four million hu, transported to supply the army.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun circularized the counties, drilled the militia, felled a million arrow-shafts from the imperial bamboo grove at Qi, raised two thousand remounts, levied four million hu of grain, and forwarded everything to the front.

sentences.zh: 朱鮪聞光武北而河內孤，使討難將軍蘇茂、副將賈彊將兵三萬餘人，度鞏河攻溫。

translations.literal: Zhu You heard Guangwu had gone north and Henei stood alone, sent General Who Strikes Difficulties Su Mao and deputy general Jia Qiang to lead troops more than thirty thousand, cross the Gong River, and attack Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu You, learning that Liu Xiu had gone north and left Henei exposed, sent Su Mao and Jia Qiang at the head of thirty thousand men across the Gong River against Wen.

sentences.zh: 檄書至，恂即勒軍馳出，並移告屬縣發兵，會於溫下。

translations.literal: When the call-up writ arrived, Xun immediately led the army galloping out, and simultaneously moved notice to the subordinate counties to raise troops, assembling below Wen.
translations.idiomatic: The moment the alert reached him, Kou Xun mustered his command, galloped out, and ordered every county to send men to a rendezvous below Wen.

sentences.zh: 軍吏皆諫曰：『今洛陽兵渡河，前後不絕，宜待眾軍畢集，乃可出也。

translations.literal: The army clerics all remonstrated, saying: 'Now Luoyang's troops cross the River, front and rear without cease; it is fitting to wait until the mass of armies is fully gathered, then one can go out.
translations.idiomatic: His officers protested: 'Luoyang's columns are still pouring across the river. We should wait until every detachment arrives.

sentences.zh: 』恂曰：『溫，郡之藩蔽，失溫則郡不可守。

translations.literal: Xun said: 'Wen is the commandery's screen; lose Wen then the commandery cannot be guarded.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun said, 'Wen is our shield. Lose Wen and we lose the whole commandery.

sentences.zh: 』遂馳赴之。

translations.literal: Thereupon he galloped to go there.
translations.idiomatic: He spurred straight for the city.

sentences.zh: 旦日合戰，而偏將軍馮異遣救，及諸縣兵適至，士馬四集，幡旗蔽野。

translations.literal: At dawn they joined battle, and Lieutenant General Feng Yi sent relief, and the troops of the various counties just arrived; soldiers and horses gathered on four sides, banners covered the wilds.
translations.idiomatic: At daybreak the lines closed. Feng Yi's reinforcements and the county levies arrived in the nick of time; standards filled the plain.

sentences.zh: 恂乃令士卒乘城，鼓噪大呼，言曰：『劉公兵到！

translations.literal: Xun thereupon ordered soldiers to mount the walls, drum and shout greatly, saying: 'Lord Liu's troops have arrived!'
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun had his men swarm the walls, beat the drums, and roar, 'Lord Liu's army is here!'

sentences.zh: 』蘇茂軍聞之，陣動，恂因奔擊，大破之，追至洛陽，遂斬賈彊。

translations.literal: Su Mao's army heard it; the formation moved; Xun thereupon charged and struck, greatly defeated them, pursued to Luoyang, thereupon beheaded Jia Qiang.
translations.idiomatic: Su Mao's ranks wavered at the cry. Kou Xun hurled his men into the breach, routed them, chased them toward Luoyang, and took Jia Qiang's head.

sentences.zh: 茂兵自投河死者數千，生獲萬餘人。

translations.literal: Mao's troops of themselves threw into the river and died several thousand; alive captured more than ten thousand.
translations.idiomatic: Thousands of Su Mao's men drowned themselves in the river; over ten thousand were taken alive.

sentences.zh: 恂與馮異過河而還。

translations.literal: Xun together with Feng Yi crossed the River and returned.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun and Feng Yi recrossed the river and withdrew.

sentences.zh: 自是，洛陽震恐，城門晝閉。

translations.literal: From this Luoyang trembled with fear; the city gates were closed by day.
translations.idiomatic: Luoyang shook with alarm and barred its gates even in daylight.

sentences.zh: 時，光武傳聞朱鮪破河內，有頃，恂檄至，大喜曰：『吾知寇子翼可任也！

translations.literal: At the time Guangwu by transmitted report heard Zhu You had broken Henei; after a moment Xun's call-up writ arrived; greatly pleased he said: 'I knew Kou Ziyi could be entrusted!'
translations.idiomatic: A courier had told Liu Xiu that Zhu You had crushed Henei; a moment later Kou Xun's dispatch arrived. He exclaimed with delight, 'I knew I could trust Kou Ziyi!'

sentences.zh: 』諸將軍賀，因上尊號，於是即位。

translations.literal: The generals congratulated him, thereupon presenting the exalted title, whereupon he ascended the throne.
translations.idiomatic: His generals pressed the congratulations and urged him to take the imperial title; he accepted and ascended the throne.

sentences.zh: 時，軍食急乏，恂以輦車驪駕轉輸，前後不絕，尚書升斗以稟百官。

translations.literal: At the time army food was urgently lacking; Xun with cart teams and black-paired teams transported in relay, front and rear without cease; the masters' office measured in sheng and dou to supply the hundred officials.
translations.idiomatic: Supplies ran desperately short at court. Kou Xun ran wagon trains of paired black horses in endless relay and measured grain out by the cupful for the bureaucracy.

sentences.zh: 帝數策書勞問，恂同門生茂陵董崇說恂曰：『上新即位，四方未定，而郡侯以此時據大郡，內得人心，外破蘇茂，威震鄰敵，功名發聞，此讒人側目怨禍之時也。

translations.literal: The emperor several times with brush writs consoled and inquired; Xun's fellow student from the same gate Maoling Dong Chong spoke to Xun, saying: 'The sovereign has newly ascended; the four quarters are not yet settled, yet the commandery marquis at this time holds a great commandery, within winning men's hearts, without breaking Su Mao, awe shaking neighboring foes—merit and name issue forth; this is the time when slanderers glare sideways and resentful disaster comes.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor sent letter after letter of praise. Dong Chong, an old schoolmate from Maoling, warned him: 'The new throne is still unsteady, yet you command a great commandery, win the people, crush Su Mao, and terrify the enemy—your fame is too bright. This is exactly when envious men sharpen their knives.'

sentences.zh: 昔蕭何守關中，悟鮑生之言而高祖悅。

translations.literal: In the past Xiao He guarded Guanzhong, comprehended Master Bao's words and the High Ancestor was pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Remember how Xiao He, guarding Guanzhong, heeded Bao Shengrui's counsel and set Gaozu's mind at ease.

sentences.zh: 今君所將，皆宗族昆弟也，無乃當以前人為鏡戒。

translations.literal: Now those you lead are all clan kin and cousins; is it not that one ought to take the former men as mirror and warning.
translations.idiomatic: Your command is packed with kinsmen; you should take those earlier men as your mirror.

sentences.zh: 』恂然其言，稱疾不視事。

translations.literal: Xun approved his words, pleaded illness, and did not attend to affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun agreed, feigned illness, and stepped back from day-to-day government.

sentences.zh: 帝將攻洛陽，先至河內，恂求從軍。

translations.literal: The emperor was about to attack Luoyang, first came to Henei; Xun begged to follow the army.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Xiu prepared the drive on Luoyang, he stopped first in Henei; Kou Xun begged to join the expedition.

sentences.zh: 帝曰：『河內未可離也。

translations.literal: The emperor said: 'Henei cannot yet be left.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, 'I still cannot spare Henei.'

sentences.zh: 』數固請，不聽，乃遣兄子寇張、姊子谷崇將突騎，願為軍鋒。

translations.literal: Several times he firmly requested; he did not listen, thereupon sent elder brother's son Kou Zhang and elder sister's son Gu Chong to lead assault cavalry, wishing to be the army's vanguard.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun pleaded again and again in vain. The emperor instead took his nephew Kou Zhang and his sister's son Gu Chong, gave them shock cavalry, and sent them ahead as his spearhead.

sentences.zh: 帝善之，皆以為偏將軍。

translations.literal: The emperor approved them, all appointing them lieutenant generals.
translations.idiomatic: He approved and named both lieutenant generals.

sentences.zh: 建武二年，恂坐系考上書者免。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianwu, Xun sat guilt for binding and examining one who submitted a memorial and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 2 Kou Xun was dismissed for jailing a man who had petitioned the throne.

sentences.zh: 是時，潁川人嚴終、趙敦聚眾萬餘，與密人賈期連兵為寇。

translations.literal: At that time men of Yingchuan Yan Zhong and Zhao Dun gathered a host of more than ten thousand, and with Mi man Jia Qi joined armies to be bandits.
translations.idiomatic: About then Yan Zhong and Zhao Dun of Yingchuan raised ten thousand followers and allied with Jia Qi of Mi to raid the countryside.

sentences.zh: 恂免數月，復拜潁川太守，與破奸將軍侯進俱擊之。

translations.literal: Several months after Xun was dismissed, again appointed grand administrator of Yingchuan, together with General Who Breaks Treachery Hou Jin jointly struck them.
translations.idiomatic: A few months after his dismissal he was again named grand administrator of Yingchuan and sent with Hou Jin, general who breaks treachery, against the rebels.

sentences.zh: 數月，斬期首，郡中悉平定。

translations.literal: Several months, beheaded Qi's head; in the commandery all was pacified.
translations.idiomatic: Within months he took Jia Qi's head and quieted the commandery.

sentences.zh: 封恂雍奴侯，邑萬戶。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed Xun as marquis of Yongnu, with ten thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: He enfeoffed Kou Xun as marquis of Yongnu with revenue from ten thousand households.

sentences.zh: 執金吾賈復在汝南，部將殺人於潁川，恂捕得系獄。

translations.literal: Bearer of the golden mace Jia Fu was in Runan; a subordinate general killed a man in Yingchuan; Xun arrested him, bound him, and put him in prison.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Fu, bearer of the golden mace, was stationed in Runan when one of his officers murdered a man in Yingchuan. Kou Xun arrested the killer and threw him in jail.

sentences.zh: 時尚草創，軍營犯法，率多相容，恂乃戮之於市。

translations.literal: At the time it was still in founding beginnings; army camps breaking the law, for the most part mutually tolerated; Xun thereupon executed him in the market.
translations.idiomatic: In those early days army lawlessness was usually winked at; Kou Xun had the man executed in the marketplace.

sentences.zh: 復以為恥，嘆。

translations.literal: Fu took it as shame, sighed.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Fu took it as a public humiliation and brooded.

sentences.zh: 還過潁川，謂左右曰：『吾與寇恂並列將帥，而今為其所陷，大丈夫豈有懷侵怨而不決之者乎？

translations.literal: Returning he passed Yingchuan, said to those at his side: 'I and Kou Xun stand ranked as generals, yet now am trapped by him—how can a great man harbor an insulting grudge and not decide it?'
translations.idiomatic: On his way back through Yingchuan he told his staff, 'Kou Xun and I are brother generals, yet he has shamed me. Can a grown man swallow an affront and do nothing?'

sentences.zh: 今見恂，必手劍之！

translations.literal: Now when I see Xun, I will surely hand-sword him!'
translations.idiomatic: The next time I see Kou Xun I will cut him down myself!'

sentences.zh: 』恂知其謀，不欲與相見。

translations.literal: Xun knew his plan, did not wish to meet with him.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun heard of the threat and avoided a meeting.

sentences.zh: 谷崇曰：『崇，將也，得帶劍侍側。

translations.literal: Gu Chong said: 'I, Chong, am a general; I may wear a sword and attend at the side.'
translations.idiomatic: Gu Chong offered, 'Let me stand at your side with a bared blade—I am a fighting man.'

sentences.zh: 卒有變，足以相當。

translations.literal: If there is suddenly a change, it is sufficient to match him.
translations.idiomatic: If he tries anything, I can answer him.

sentences.zh: 』恂曰：『不然。

translations.literal: Xun said: 'It is not so.'
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun said, 'No.'

sentences.zh: 昔藺相如不畏秦王而屈於廉頗者，為國也。

translations.literal: In the past Lin Xiangru did not fear the King of Qin yet yielded to Lian Po—this was for the state.
translations.idiomatic: Lin Xiangru defied the King of Qin yet bowed to Lian Po—for the sake of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 區區之趙，尚有此義，吾安可以忘之乎？

translations.literal: The petty Zhao still had this righteousness; how can I forget it?
translations.idiomatic: Even the petty state of Zhao understood that duty; how could I forget it?'

sentences.zh: 』乃敕屬縣盛供具，儲酒醪，執金吾軍入界，一人皆兼二人之饌。

translations.literal: Thereupon he ordered the subordinate counties to prepare lavish provisions, store ale mash; when the bearer of the golden mace army entered the boundary, each man was given rations for two.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered every county to lay in a double feast and brew ale. When Jia Fu's column crossed the border, every soldier found rations waiting for two men.

sentences.zh: 恂乃出迎於道，稱疾而還。

translations.literal: Xun thereupon went out to welcome on the road, claimed illness, and returned.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun rode out to greet Jia Fu, then doubled over with a sudden 'illness' and rode home.

sentences.zh: 賈復勒兵欲追之，而使士皆醉，遂過去。

translations.literal: Jia Fu led troops wishing to pursue him, but caused the soldiers all to be drunk, thereupon passed by.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Fu tried to give chase, but his men were hopelessly drunk and the column rolled on.

sentences.zh: 恂遣谷崇以狀聞，帝乃征恂。

translations.literal: Xun sent Gu Chong to report the situation to the emperor; the emperor thereupon summoned Xun.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun sent Gu Chong to explain matters at court; the emperor summoned Kou Xun to the capital.

sentences.zh: 恂至引見，時復先在坐，欲起相避。

translations.literal: When Xun arrived and was led to audience, at the time Fu was first seated and wished to rise to mutually avoid.
translations.idiomatic: At the audience Jia Fu was already there and started to rise to keep his distance.

sentences.zh: 帝曰：『天下未定，兩虎安得私鬥？

translations.literal: The emperor said: 'The realm is not yet settled; how can two tigers privately fight?'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, 'The realm is still unsettled—must my two tigers claw each other?'

sentences.zh: 今日朕分之。

translations.literal: Today I divide you.
translations.idiomatic: Today I settle the matter between you.

sentences.zh: 』於是並坐極歡，遂共車同出，結友而去。

translations.literal: Thereupon they sat together with utmost joy, thereupon shared a chariot and went out together, knotted friendship and departed.
translations.idiomatic: He made them sit together, drink their fill, ride out in the same carriage, and part as sworn friends.

sentences.zh: 恂歸潁川。

translations.literal: Xun returned to Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun went back to Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 三年，遣使者即拜為汝南太守，又使驃騎將軍杜茂將兵助恂討盜賊。

translations.literal: In the third year an envoy was dispatched to appoint him on the spot grand administrator of Runan, again sent General of Swift Cavalry Du Mao to lead troops to assist Xun in striking bandits.
translations.idiomatic: In the third year an envoy invested him as grand administrator of Runan and detached Du Mao, general of swift cavalry, to help him clear the bandits.

sentences.zh: 盜賊清靜，郡中無事。

translations.literal: Bandits were cleared and quiet; in the commandery there was nothing to do.
translations.idiomatic: The bandits melted away and the commandery knew peace.

sentences.zh: 恂素好學，乃修鄉校，教生徒，聘能為《左氏春秋》者，親受學焉。

translations.literal: Xun by nature loved learning; thereupon he repaired village schools, taught students, hired one able in the Zuo tradition of the Spring and Autumn, personally received study from him.
translations.idiomatic: A scholar by temperament, he rebuilt the local academy, enrolled pupils, and hired a master of the Zuo tradition of the Spring and Autumn, whom he studied under himself.

sentences.zh: 七年，代朱浮為執金吾。

translations.literal: In the seventh year he replaced Zhu Fu as bearer of the golden mace.
translations.idiomatic: In the seventh year he succeeded Zhu Fu as bearer of the golden mace.

sentences.zh: 明年，從車駕擊隗囂，而潁川盜賊群起，帝乃引軍還，謂恂曰：『潁川迫近京師，當以時定。

translations.literal: The next year he followed the imperial carriage in striking Wei Ao, but Yingchuan bandits rose in swarms; the emperor thereupon led the army back, said to Xun: 'Yingchuan presses close on the capital region—it ought to be settled in due time.'
translations.idiomatic: The next year he joined the emperor against Wei Ao, but Yingchuan exploded in revolt. Liu Xiu wheeled about and told Kou Xun, 'Yingchuan sits on the capital's doorstep—it must be pacified soon.'

sentences.zh: 惟念獨卿能平之耳，從九卿復出，以憂國可也。

translations.literal: Only I think you alone can pacify it; from the nine ministers again to go out, for grieving the state, is acceptable.
translations.idiomatic: Only you can settle it. I will gladly send you back from the nine ministers' bench if you will shoulder this for the realm.

sentences.zh: 』恂對曰：『潁川剽輕，聞陛下遠逾阻險，有事隴、蜀，故狂狡乘間相詿誤耳。

translations.literal: Xun replied, saying: 'Yingchuan is rash and light; hearing Your Majesty has gone far, crossing peril and obstruction, having affairs in Long and Shu, therefore the mad and crafty seize the interval mutually to deceive and lead astray.'
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun answered, 'Yingchuan folk are volatile. They heard that you had marched deep into danger in Long and Shu and thought they could exploit your absence with wild rumors.'

sentences.zh: 如聞乘輿南向，賊必惶怖歸死。

translations.literal: If they hear the imperial carriage has turned south, the bandits will surely panic and return to await death.
translations.idiomatic: Show them your chariot wheels turning south and every rebel will crawl back to beg for death.

sentences.zh: 臣願執銳前驅。

translations.literal: Your subject wishes to grasp a sharp weapon and ride vanguard.
translations.idiomatic: Let me take spear and reins and ride in the van.

sentences.zh: 』即日車駕南征，恂從至潁川，盜賊悉降，而竟不拜郡。

translations.literal: That day the imperial carriage south campaigned; Xun followed to Yingchuan; bandits all surrendered, yet in the end he was not appointed to the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor marched south the same day; Kou Xun rode with him to Yingchuan, where every bandit submitted—yet he was never formally reappointed to the post.

sentences.zh: 百姓遮道曰：『願從陛下復借寇君一年。

translations.literal: The hundred surnames blocked the road, saying: 'We wish from Your Majesty again to borrow Director Kou for one year.'
translations.idiomatic: The people blocked the road, crying, 'Lend us Director Kou for one more year.'

sentences.zh: 』乃留恂長社，鎮撫使人，受納餘降。

translations.literal: Thereupon he left Xun at Changshe, pacified and soothed the people, received and took in the remaining who surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor left him at Changshe to calm the district and process the last surrenders.

sentences.zh: 初，隗囂將安定高峻，擁兵萬人，據高平第一，帝使待詔馬援招降峻，由是河西道開。

translations.literal: Earlier, Wei Ao's general Gao Jun of Anding, holding troops ten thousand, occupied Gaoping first; the emperor sent awaiting edict Ma Yuan to summon and surrender Jun, whereupon the Hexi road opened.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier Gao Jun of Anding, a general under Wei Ao, had held ten thousand men on the Gaoping heights. The emperor sent Ma Yuan to talk him down and open the Hexi road.

sentences.zh: 中郎將來歙承制拜峻通路將軍，封關內侯，後屬大司馬吳漢，共圍囂於冀。

translations.literal: General of the Gentlemen of the Household Lai Xi, acting on imperial authority, appointed Jun general who opens the road, enfeoffed marquis within the passes, later attached to Grand Marshal Wu Han, together besieging Ao at Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Lai Xi then commissioned Gao Jun as general who opens the road and marquis within the passes; later he served under Wu Han in the siege of Wei Ao at Ji.

sentences.zh: 及漢軍退，峻亡歸故營，復助囂拒隴阺。

translations.literal: When Han's army withdrew, Jun fled back to the old camp, again assisted Ao in resisting Longzhi.
translations.idiomatic: When Han forces pulled back, Gao Jun fled to his old fort and again helped Wei Ao hold the Long defile.

sentences.zh: 及囂死，峻據高平，畏誅堅守。

translations.literal: When Ao died, Jun held Gaoping, feared execution, and firmly guarded.
translations.idiomatic: After Wei Ao died Gao Jun still held Gaoping, terrified of execution and refusing to yield.

sentences.zh: 建威大將軍耿弇率太中大夫竇士、武威太守梁統等圍之，一歲不拔。

translations.literal: General Who Establishes Might Geng Yan led grandee for splendid counsel Dou Shi, grand administrator of Wuwei Liang Tong, and others besieging him, one year could not take him.
translations.idiomatic: Geng Yan, general who establishes might, besieged him with Dou Shi, Liang Tong, and the rest for a year without success.

sentences.zh: 十年，帝入關，將自征之，恂時從駕，諫曰：『長安道里居中，應接近便，安定、隴西必懷震懼，此從容一處可以制四方也。

translations.literal: In the tenth year the emperor entered the passes, was about to personally campaign against him; Xun at the time followed the carriage and remonstrated, saying: 'Chang'an's roads and li stand in the center, responding and drawing near is convenient; Anding and Longxi are sure to harbor shock and fear—this from one relaxed place can control the four quarters.'
translations.idiomatic: In the tenth year the emperor entered Guanzhong intending to lead the assault in person. Kou Xun, riding in the train, urged him to stay at Chang'an: 'The capital sits at the hub; a show of strength here will unnerve Anding and Longxi and let you command the west without marching every mile yourself.'

sentences.zh: 今士馬疲倦，方履險阻，非萬乘之固，前年潁川，可為至戒。

translations.literal: Now soldiers and horses are weary and tired, just treading peril and obstruction—this is not the firmness of the ten-thousand-chariot host; the year before last in Yingchuan can serve as utmost warning.
translations.idiomatic: Your troops are exhausted and the terrain deadly—no place for the Son of Heaven to gamble. Remember the Yingchuan scare two years ago.

sentences.zh: 』帝不從。

translations.literal: The emperor did not follow.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor would not listen.

sentences.zh: 進軍及汧，峻猶不下，帝議遣使降之，乃謂恂曰：『卿前止吾此舉，今為吾行也。

translations.literal: Advancing the army reached Qian; Jun still would not submit; the emperor deliberated sending an envoy to summon his surrender, thereupon said to Xun: 'You before stopped me in this move; now for me go.'
translations.idiomatic: The army reached Qian; Gao Jun still held out. The emperor decided to try diplomacy and told Kou Xun, 'You warned me against this campaign—now go finish it for me.'

sentences.zh: 若峻不即降，引耿弇等五營擊之。

translations.literal: If Jun does not immediately surrender, lead Geng Yan and others of the five camps to strike him.'
translations.idiomatic: If he will not yield at once, bring Geng Yan and the five camps down on him.'

sentences.zh: 』恂奉璽書至第一，峻遣軍師皇甫文出謁，辭禮不屈。

translations.literal: Xun bearing the imperial jade writ arrived at First; Jun sent army director Huangfu Wen out to meet, in words and ritual not yielding.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun brought the sealed edict to Gao Jun's stronghold at First. Jun sent his strategist Huangfu Wen to the parley; Wen was defiant in word and bearing.

sentences.zh: 恂怒，將誅文。

translations.literal: Xun was angry and was about to execute Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun flew into a rage and ordered Wen beheaded.

sentences.zh: 諸將諫曰：『高峻精兵萬人，率多強弩，西遮隴道，連年不下。

translations.literal: The generals remonstrated, saying: 'Gao Jun's crack troops are ten thousand, for the most part strong crossbows, west blocking the Long road, for successive years not taken.'
translations.idiomatic: His officers protested: 'Gao Jun has ten thousand picked men, mostly heavy crossbowmen, blocking the Long road. We have failed to reduce him for years.'

sentences.zh: 今欲降之而反戮其使，無乃不可乎？

translations.literal: Now you wish to summon his surrender yet in turn kill his envoy—is this not impermissible?'
translations.idiomatic: You mean to talk him down and then murder his spokesman—can that be a wise move?'

sentences.zh: 』恂不應，遂斬之。

translations.literal: Xun did not respond, thereupon beheaded him.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun said nothing and had Wen executed.

sentences.zh: 遣其副歸告峻曰：『軍師無禮，已戮之矣。

translations.literal: He sent his deputy back to report to Jun, saying: 'The army director was rude without propriety; I have already executed him.'
translations.idiomatic: He sent Wen's second-in-command back to Gao Jun with the word: 'Your strategist broke every rule of courtesy; I have put him to death.'

sentences.zh: 欲降，急降；

translations.literal: If you wish to surrender, quickly surrender;
translations.idiomatic: If you intend to yield, do it now;

sentences.zh: 不欲，固守。

translations.literal: if you do not wish, firmly guard.'
translations.idiomatic: if not, dig in and fight.'

sentences.zh: 』峻惶恐，即日開城門降。

translations.literal: Jun was fearful and panicked; that day opened the city gates and surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: Gao Jun panicked and opened his gates that same day.

sentences.zh: 諸將皆賀，因曰：『敢問殺其使而降其城，何也？

translations.literal: The generals all congratulated him, thereupon saying: 'We venture to ask—killing his envoy yet bringing his city to surrender—why was this?'
translations.idiomatic: The generals cheered, then asked, 'How could killing the envoy have won the city?'

sentences.zh: 』恂曰：『皇甫文，峻之腹心，其所取計者也。

translations.literal: Xun said: 'Huangfu Wen was Jun's belly and heart, the one from whom he took his plans.'
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun answered, 'Huangfu Wen was the brain of Gao Jun; every plan ran through him.'

sentences.zh: 今來，辭意不屈，必無降心。

translations.literal: Now that he came, his words and intent did not yield—surely there was no mind to surrender.'
translations.idiomatic: When he came he was still truculent—he had no intention of yielding.'

sentences.zh: 全之則文得其計，殺之則峻亡其膽，是以降耳。

translations.literal: If one spared him then Wen would obtain his plan; killing him then Jun lost his gall—therefore he surrendered.'
translations.idiomatic: Let him live and his counsel still wins the day; cut him down and Gao Jun loses his nerve—that is why the gates opened.'

sentences.zh: 』諸將皆曰：『非所及也。

translations.literal: The generals all said: 'This is not what we could have reached.'
translations.idiomatic: The generals admitted, 'None of us would have seen that far.'

sentences.zh: 』遂傳峻還洛陽。

translations.literal: Thereupon they sent Jun back to Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: They then escorted Gao Jun to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 恂經明行修，名重朝廷，所得秩奉，厚施朋友、故人及從吏士。

translations.literal: Xun was clear in the classics and cultivated in conduct; his name weighed heavily at court; what he received in salary and stipend he generously gave to friends, old acquaintances, and attendant clerks and soldiers.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Xun mastered the canon and lived an exemplary life; the capital esteemed him. He poured his official income into the hands of friends, old comrades, and his staff.

sentences.zh: 常曰：『吾因士大夫以致此，其可獨享之乎！

translations.literal: He always said: 'I reached this through the gentlemen of the realm—how can I enjoy it alone!'
translations.idiomatic: He used to say, 'The scholar-officials lifted me to this height—how could I possibly enjoy it alone!'

sentences.zh: 』時人歸其長者，以為有宰相器。

translations.literal: Men of the time returned him as a venerable elder, considering him to have the capacity of a minister.
translations.idiomatic: Contemporaries called him a true elder statesman and said he had the makings of a chief minister.

sentences.zh: 十二年卒，謚曰威侯。

translations.literal: In the twelfth year he died; his posthumous title was Marquis Wei.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the twelfth year of the reign with the posthumous name Wei, the awe-inspiring.

sentences.zh: 子損嗣。

translations.literal: His son Sun succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Kou Sun inherited the title.

sentences.zh: 恂同產弟及兄子、姊子以軍功封列侯者凡八人，終其身，不傳於後。

translations.literal: Xun's younger brothers by the same birth and elder brother's sons and elder sister's sons who by military merit were enfeoffed as full marquises numbered eight in all; for their whole lives they did not transmit to posterity.
translations.idiomatic: Eight of Kou Xun's full brothers, nephews, and sister's sons won full marquisates on campaign; the emperor let each hold the honor for life without passing it down.

sentences.zh: 初所與謀閔業者，恂數為帝言其忠，賜爵關內侯，官至遼西太守。

translations.literal: The one with whom he first plotted, Min Ye—Xun several times for the emperor spoke of his loyalty, granted him noble rank as marquis within the passes, and his office reached grand administrator of Liaoxi.
translations.idiomatic: Min Ye, who had first plotted with him, Kou Xun repeatedly praised to the throne; the court enfeoffed him as marquis within the passes and eventually named him grand administrator of Liaoxi.

sentences.zh: 十三年，復封損庶兄壽為洨侯。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year he again enfeoffed Sun's elder brother by a concubine Shou as marquis of Jiao.
translations.idiomatic: In the thirteenth year the emperor also made Kou Shou, Sun's elder half-brother, marquis of Jiao.

sentences.zh: 後徙封損扶柳侯。

translations.literal: Later he transferred Sun's fief to marquis of Foliu.
translations.idiomatic: Later Kou Sun's fief was moved to the marquisate of Foliu.

sentences.zh: 損卒，子釐嗣，徙封商鄉侯。

translations.literal: When Sun died, his son Li succeeded, transferred fief to marquis of Shangxiang.
translations.idiomatic: When Sun died his son Kou Li inherited the line and was shifted to the marquisate of Shangxiang.

sentences.zh: 釐卒，子襲嗣。

translations.literal: When Li died, his son Xi succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: When Li died his son Kou Xi succeeded.

sentences.zh: 恂女孫為大將軍鄧騭夫人，由是寇氏得志於永初間。

translations.literal: Xun's granddaughter was wife to Grand General Deng Zhi; thereby the Kou clan had its way in the Yongchu years.
translations.idiomatic: A granddaughter of Kou Xun married Grand General Deng Zhi, which gave the Kous their moment of power during Yongchu.

sentences.zh: 恂曾孫榮。

translations.literal: Xun's great-grandson Rong.
translations.idiomatic: His great-grandson was Kou Rong.

sentences.zh: 論曰：傳稱『喜怒以類者鮮矣』。

translations.literal: The discussion says: The tradition states 'Those who rejoice and anger according to kind are few.'
translations.idiomatic: The historian remarks: The classic says few men keep joy and anger in their proper measure.

sentences.zh: 夫喜而不比，怒而思難者，其惟君子乎！

translations.literal: As for rejoicing yet not banding together, angering yet thinking of difficulty—perhaps only the gentleman!'
translations.idiomatic: To rejoice without cliquishness and to rage yet still weigh the danger—perhaps that is the mark of the gentleman!'

sentences.zh: 子曰：『伯夷、叔齊，不念舊惡，怨是用希。

translations.literal: The Master said: 'Bo Yi and Shu Qi did not remember old wrongs; therefore resentments toward them were rare.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said of the brothers Bo Yi and Shu Qi that they nursed no old grudges, and so drew little hatred.

sentences.zh: 』於寇公而見之矣。

translations.literal: In Lord Kou one sees it.'
translations.idiomatic: The same temper appears in Lord Kou.'

sentences.zh: 榮少知名，桓帝時為侍中。

translations.literal: Rong in youth had a reputation; in Emperor Huan's time he was palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Kou Rong was famous while young and served as palace attendant under Emperor Huan.

sentences.zh: 性矜潔自貴，於人少所與，以此見害於權寵。

translations.literal: By nature he was proud, clean, and self-esteemed; toward people he seldom granted intimacy; by this he was harmed by the powerful and favored.
translations.idiomatic: He was icy, fastidious, and convinced of his own worth, granting friendship to few—and the mighty came to hate him for it.

sentences.zh: 而從兄子尚帝妹益陽長公主，帝又聘其從孫女於後宮，左右益惡之。

translations.literal: Yet his elder cousin's son married the emperor's younger sister the Princess of Yiyang; the emperor also took his great-granddaughter by a cousin into the rear palace; those about the throne hated him the more.
translations.idiomatic: His cousin's son wed the Princess of Yiyang, the emperor's sister, while another cousin's great-granddaughter entered the harem; court favorites loathed him twice over.

sentences.zh: 延熹中，遂陷以罪辟，與宗族免歸故郡。

translations.literal: In the Yanxi period they thereupon trapped him with a crime and dismissed him; together with the kin group he was dismissed and returned to his old commandery.
translations.idiomatic: During Yanxi they framed him, stripped his office, and sent the whole clan home to their native commandery.

sentences.zh: 吏承望風旨，持之浸急，榮恐不免，奔闕自訟。

translations.literal: The clerks, catching the drift of their superiors' intent, pressed him ever harder; Rong feared he could not escape, ran to the capital gate to plead his own case.
translations.idiomatic: Local officials, scenting high policy, tightened the screws until Rong feared the worst and fled toward the capital to petition in person.

sentences.zh: 未至，刺史張敬追劾榮以擅去邊，有詔捕之。

translations.literal: Before he arrived, Regional Inspector Zhang Jing pursued and impeached Rong for leaving the frontier without authority; there was an edict to arrest him.
translations.idiomatic: Before he reached Luoyang, Inspector Zhang Jing charged him with abandoning his post; an edict went out for his arrest.

sentences.zh: 榮逃竄數年，會赦令，不得除，積窮困，乃自亡命中上書曰：

translations.literal: Rong fled and hid several years; it happened that amnesty orders came, yet he could not be cleared; accumulating destitution, thereupon from his life-on-the-run submitted a memorial, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Kou Rong hid for years. The general amnesties passed but never cleared his name. Driven to desperation, he risked his life to submit this memorial:

sentences.zh: 臣聞天地之於萬物也好生，帝王之於萬人也慈愛。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that Heaven and Earth toward the myriad things love life; the Son of Heaven toward the myriad people cherishes and loves.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that Heaven and Earth love all that lives, and that the true king loves his people.

sentences.zh: 陛下統天理物，為萬國覆，作人父母，先慈愛，後威武，先寬容，後刑辟，自生齒以上，咸蒙德澤。

translations.literal: Your Majesty orders Heaven and manages things, is the covering for the myriad states, acts as father and mother to men—first cherishing and loving, afterward awe-inspiring might; first lenient tolerance, afterward punishments; from those who have grown teeth upward, all receive virtuous moisture.
translations.idiomatic: You align with Heaven, shelter every kingdom, and are father and mother to the people—kindness before terror, mercy before the law, so that every grown soul drinks your grace.

sentences.zh: 而臣兄弟獨以無辜為專權之臣所見批扺，青蠅之人所共構會。

translations.literal: Yet your subject's brothers alone, for no crime, were struck and pushed aside by ministers who monopolize power, jointly plotted against by men who swarm like green flies.
translations.idiomatic: Yet my brothers alone, though guiltless, were shoved aside by a clique in power and slandered by every petty whisperer at court.

sentences.zh: 以臣婚姻王室，謂臣將撫其背，奪其位，退其身，受其執。

translations.literal: Because your subject's marriage ties him to the royal house, they say your subject would stroke their backs, seize their places, force them aside, and take their grip.
translations.idiomatic: Because I married into the Liu house, they accuse me of plotting to pat their backs, steal their seats, elbow them out, and seize their hold on power.

sentences.zh: 於是遂作飛章以被於臣，欲使墜萬仞之坑，踐必死之地，令陛下忽慈母之仁，發投杼之怒。

translations.literal: Thereupon they made flying accusations to cover your subject, wishing to cast him into a pit ten thousand ren deep, tread ground where death is certain, making Your Majesty suddenly forget a loving mother's kindness and unleash the fury of the 'throwing the shuttle' tale.
translations.idiomatic: They invented flying indictments to bury me, hoping to drop me into an abyss and stamp me into certain death—hoping you would forget a mother's mercy and believe a stepmother's lie, like the story of Zengzi's mother.

sentences.zh: 尚書背繩墨，案空劾，不復質確其過，置於嚴棘之下，便奏正臣罪。

translations.literal: The masters of writing turned their backs on the plumb line, examined empty impeachments, no longer verified their errors, placed them beneath harsh brambles, then memorialized to fix your subject's crime as settled.
translations.idiomatic: The secretariat abandoned straight measure, rubber-stamped empty charges, skipped any real inquiry, threw us on the thorns, and rushed to declare us convicted.

sentences.zh: 司隸校尉馮羨佞邪承旨，廢於王命，驅逐臣等，不得旅踵。

translations.literal: Colonel of the Metropolitan Region Feng Xian, fawning and crooked, received the drift of edicts, cast aside the king's command, drove your subject and others so they could not set heel to heel.
translations.idiomatic: Colonel Feng Xian of the Metropolitan Region, a toady who bent every edict to malice, cast the royal command aside and harried us until we could not stand our ground.

sentences.zh: 臣奔走還郡，沒齒無怨。

translations.literal: Your subject ran back to the commandery; to the end of his teeth he has no resentment.
translations.idiomatic: I fled home to my commandery and bore it without complaint.

sentences.zh: 臣誠恐卒為豺狼橫見噬食，故冒死欲詣闕，披肝膽，布腹心。

translations.literal: Your subject truly fears that in the end he will be horizontally seen devoured by jackals and wolves; therefore braving death wishes to reach the gate, spread liver and gall, lay out belly and heart.
translations.idiomatic: I truly fear I will yet be torn apart by the wolves in office; therefore I risk death to reach your gate and lay my heart bare before you.

sentences.zh: 刺史張敬，好為諂諛，張設機網，復令陛下興雷電之怒。

translations.literal: Regional Inspector Zhang Jing loves to flatter; he spreads nets and snares, again making Your Majesty raise thunder-and-lightning wrath.
translations.idiomatic: The Inspector Zhang Jing lives for flattery; he weaves traps until you thunder against us again.

sentences.zh: 司隸校尉應奉、河南尹何豹、洛陽令袁騰並驅爭先，若赴仇敵，罰及死沒，髡剔墳墓，但未掘壙出屍，剖棺露胔耳。

translations.literal: Colonel of the Metropolitan Region Ying Feng, grand administrator of Henan He Bao, and magistrate of Luoyuan Yuan Teng together drove and vied to be first, as if rushing on sworn foes; punishment reached the dead and buried, shaving and plundering tombs—only they have not yet dug the vaults to drag out corpses, split coffins, and expose the bones.
translations.idiomatic: Colonel Ying Feng, Administrator He Bao, and Magistrate Yuan Teng raced one another like avengers, punishing the long dead, razoring grave mounds, and looting tombs—they have stopped just short of wrenching bodies from the earth and breaking open coffins.

sentences.zh: 昔文王葬枯骨，公劉敦行葦，世稱其仁。

translations.literal: In the past King Wen buried dry bones; Duke Liu was kind to reeds and rushes; the age praised their benevolence.
translations.idiomatic: King Wen gathered scattered bones for burial; Duke Liu spared the marsh grasses; the world called them humane.

sentences.zh: 今殘酷容媚之吏，無折中處平之心，不顧不辜之害，而興虛誣之誹，欲使嚴朝必加濫罰。

translations.literal: Now cruel and fawning clerks, without a mind for the balanced middle, heedless of the harm to the guiltless, raise empty and false slander, wishing to make the stern court surely apply excessive punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Today cruel sycophants, caring nothing for justice or the innocent, traffic in lies so the court will crush us without measure.

sentences.zh: 是以不敢觸突天威，而自竄山林，以俊陛下發神聖之聽。

translations.literal: Therefore I dare not collide with Heaven's majesty, but hide myself in mountain forests, waiting for Your Majesty to extend sacred hearing.
translations.idiomatic: So I dare not defy your majesty face to face; I hide in the hills until you will listen with sacred ears.

sentences.zh: 啟獨睹之明，拒逸慝之謗，絕邪巧之言，救可濟之人，援沒溺之命。

translations.literal: Open alone-seeing brightness, reject the slander of the runaway wicked, cut off the words of the crooked and clever, save those who can be helped, pull up those drowning in the depths.
translations.idiomatic: Let your lone clarity shine; reject fleeing villains' lies; silence clever tongues; save whom you can and haul the drowning from the water.

sentences.zh: 不意滯怒不為春夏息，淹恚不為順時怠，遂馳使郵驛，布告錠近，嚴文克剝，痛於霜雪，張羅海內，設罝萬里，逐臣者窮人跡，追臣者極車軌，雖楚購伍員，漢求季布，無以過也。

translations.literal: I did not expect pent wrath not to rest with spring and summer, long-held hatred not to slacken with the seasons; thereupon swift envoys on post-roads spread notice near and far, harsh documents flay like frost and snow, spread nets within the seas, set snares for ten thousand li; those who pursue your subject exhaust human tracks, those who chase your subject fill every wheel-rut—even Chu hunting Wu Yuan, Han seeking Ji Bu, did not surpass this.
translations.idiomatic: I never thought your anger would outlast the seasons, or that hatred would never cool—couriers race the post roads, edicts flay like frost, nets stretch across the realm, traps gird ten thousand li; hunters stamp out my footprints and choke every road. Not even the manhunts for Wu Zixu or Ji Bu were fiercer.

sentences.zh: 臣遇罰以來，三赦再贖，無驗之罪，足以蠲除。

translations.literal: From the time your subject met punishment, three amnesties and two redemptions have passed; crimes without evidence are enough to be wiped away.
translations.idiomatic: Since my sentence, the three general pardons and the two commutations have passed; unproved guilt should long ago have been expunged.

sentences.zh: 而陛下疾臣愈深，有司咎臣甫力，止則見掃滅，行則為亡虜，茍生則為窮人，極死則為冤鬼，天廣而無以自覆，地厚而無以自載，蹈陸土而有沈淪之憂，遠巖墻而有鎮壓之患。

translations.literal: Yet Your Majesty's hatred of your subject grows deeper; the responsible officials blame your subject with all their force—if he stops he is seen as wiped out, if he moves he becomes a fugitive; if he merely lives he is utterly poor, if he dies to the limit he is a wronged ghost; Heaven is wide yet he has nothing to cover himself, Earth is thick yet he has nothing to carry himself; treading dry land he yet has the worry of sinking, keeping far from earthen walls he yet has the anxiety of being crushed.
translations.idiomatic: Yet your wrath only deepens; the ministry hounds me without rest—stand still and I am erased, move and I am a runaway; live and I am destitute, die and I am a vengeful ghost. The sky is vast but gives me no roof, the earth is deep but no footing; I walk solid ground yet feel I am drowning, I flee the wall yet fear it will fall on me.

sentences.zh: 精誠足以感於陛下，而哲王未肯悟。

translations.literal: Sincere earnestness is enough to move Your Majesty, yet the wise king has not been willing to awaken.
translations.idiomatic: Such loyalty ought to move you, yet the wise king will not wake.

sentences.zh: 如臣犯元惡大憝，足以陳於原野，備刀鋸，陛下當班布臣之所坐，以解眾論之疑。

translations.literal: If your subject had committed the greatest evil and chief villainy, enough to be laid out in the open field and prepared for knife and saw, Your Majesty ought to publish your subject's crimes to dispel the crowd's doubts.
translations.idiomatic: If I had truly committed capital crimes fit for the execution ground, you should publish my guilt and silence every rumor.

sentences.zh: 臣思入國門，坐於胏石之上，使三槐九棘平臣之罪。

translations.literal: Your subject wishes to enter the state gate, sit on the lung stone, and let the three huai and nine thorns judge your subject's crime.
translations.idiomatic: I wish to pass the capital gate, sit on the accusation stone, and let the high court trees try my case.

sentences.zh: 而閶闔九重，陷阱步設，舉趾觸罘罝，動行絓羅網，無緣至萬乘之前，永無見信之期矣。

translations.literal: Yet the Gate of Chang is ninefold deep, pit and trap are set at every step; lifting a foot touches the spring-gun, moving a step snags the net; there is no way to reach before the ten-thousand-chariot host, forever no period when trust will be seen.
translations.idiomatic: But the Forbidden City's gates are nine layers deep, every step is a pit; I trip spring-guns and tangle in nets—I can never reach the Son of Heaven, never win a hearing.

sentences.zh: 國君不可仇匹夫，仇之則一國盡懼。

translations.literal: A state lord cannot bear a grudge against a common man; if he bears a grudge then the whole state is afraid.
translations.idiomatic: A prince should not nurse hatred against a commoner; if he does, the whole realm trembles.

sentences.zh: 臣奔走以來，三離寒暑，陰陽易位，當暖反寒，春常淒風，夏降霜雹，又連年大風，折拔樹木。
