translations.literal: From the time your subject ran, he has thrice left cold and heat; Yin and Yang have changed places—when it should be warm it turns cold, in spring there are always bitter winds, in summer fall hailstones; again for successive years great winds have broken and uprooted trees.
translations.idiomatic: Since I fled I have outlasted three winters and summers; the seasons themselves seem reversed—warmth turns to chill, spring brings cruel winds, summer drops hail; year after year gales snap trees.

sentences.zh: 風為號令，春夏布德，議獄緩死之時。

translations.literal: Wind is the signal of commands; spring and summer spread virtue; it is the season for deliberating cases and slowing death.
translations.idiomatic: Wind is the voice of policy; spring and summer are when mercy should spread—when courts review cases and stay executions.

sentences.zh: 願陛下思帝堯五教在寬之德，企成湯避遠讒夫之誠，以寧風旱，以弭災兵。

translations.literal: I beg Your Majesty to ponder Emperor Yao's virtue that the five teachings lie in leniency, to stand in King Cheng-Tang's sincerity of keeping slanderers at a distance, thereby to quiet wind and drought and to stop weapons of disaster.
translations.idiomatic: I beg you to remember Yao's teaching that the five moral lessons rest on mercy, and Tang's resolve to banish whisperers—so wind and drought may cease and the arms of calamity fall silent.

sentences.zh: 臣聞勇者不逃死，智者不重困，固不為明朝惜垂盡之命，願赴湘、沅之波，從屈原之悲，沈江湖之流，吊子胥之哀，臣功臣苗緒，生長王國，懼獨含恨以葬江魚之腹，無以自別於世，不勝狐死首丘之情，營魂識路之懷。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that the brave do not flee death, the wise do not double distress; therefore I do not for a clear-sighted morning spare a life on the edge of extinction, but wish to throw myself into the waves of Xiang and Yuan, follow Qu Yuan's grief, sink into the currents of the rivers, mourn Wu Zixu's sorrow—I, a sprig of a meritorious minister's line, reared in the royal domain, fear only to swallow hatred in the bellies of river fish with no way to distinguish myself in the world, unable to bear the feeling of the fox that dies with its head toward its mound, the soul that knows the road home.
translations.idiomatic: They say the brave do not run from death nor the wise court double jeopardy—so I will not cling to this flickering life. I would rather drown in the Xiang and Yuan with Qu Yuan's sorrow, sink in the great rivers with Wu Zixu's grief. I am only a twig of a house that served the throne; I dread dying full of resentment in a fish's belly, unknown to the world, unable to bear the fox's longing for its hill or the soul's need to find its road home.

sentences.zh: 犯冒王怒，觸突帝禁，伏於兩觀，陳訴毒痛，然後登金鑊，入沸湯，糜爛子熾爨之下，九死而未悔。

translations.literal: I offend Your Majesty's wrath, collide with the imperial prohibition, prostrate between the two watchtowers and state the venom of my pain, then mount the golden cauldron, enter boiling water, rot and cook beneath the blazing stove—nine deaths yet I would not regret.
translations.idiomatic: Let me offend your wrath, breach the palace ban, fall between the twin towers and pour out my poison, then climb into the bronze cauldron and the boiling cauldron—let me die nine deaths and feel no regret.

sentences.zh: 悲夫，久生亦復何卿！

translations.literal: Alas! Long life—what further use is it!'
translations.idiomatic: Alas—what good is a long life now!'

sentences.zh: 蓋忠臣殺身以解君怒，孝子殞命以寧親怨，故大舜不避塗廩浚井之難，甲生不辭姬氏讒邪之謗。

translations.literal: For loyal ministers kill themselves to dispel the ruler's anger; filial sons destroy their lives to quiet a parent's resentment—therefore great Shun did not avoid the difficulties of the granary and the well, Jiasheng did not decline the slander of the Ji clan.'
translations.idiomatic: Loyal ministers die to cool a sovereign's rage; filial sons die to soothe a parent's spite—so Shun braved the granary and the well, and Prince Shen Sheng did not flee the Jis' slanders.'

sentences.zh: 臣敢忘斯議，不自斃以解明朝之忿哉！

translations.literal: How dare your subject forget this maxim and not destroy himself to dispel the clear-sighted morning's resentment!'
translations.idiomatic: How dare I forget that lesson and refuse to give my life to appease the court's wrath!'

sentences.zh: 乞以身塞重責。

translations.literal: I beg to use my body to block the heavy blame.'
translations.idiomatic: Let my body answer for the worst of the charge.'

sentences.zh: 願陛下丐兄弟死命，使臣一門頗有遺類，以崇陛下寬饒之惠。

translations.literal: I beg Your Majesty to spare my brothers' lives of death, to let your subject's one gate still have survivors left, thereby to exalt Your Majesty's bounty of lenience and indulgence.'
translations.idiomatic: Spare the lives of my brothers so one branch of my house may survive, and magnify your reputation for mercy.'

sentences.zh: 先死陳情，臨章涕泣，泣血漣如。

translations.literal: First dying I state my feeling; facing the memorial I weep tears; my blood falls like strings.'
translations.idiomatic: I state this as one already dead; as I write I weep blood.

sentences.zh: 帝省章愈怒，遂誅榮。

translations.literal: The emperor read the memorial and grew angrier, thereupon executed Rong.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read the petition and only grew more furious; he had Kou Rong killed.

sentences.zh: 寇氏由是衰廢。

translations.literal: Thereby the Kou clan declined and was cast aside.
translations.idiomatic: The Kou clan then fell into ruin.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：元侯淵謨，乃作司徒。

translations.literal: The encomium says: The marquis of Yuan was deep in counsel, and so became minister of education.
translations.idiomatic: The verse says: The Yuan marquis counseled from the depths and rose to grand minister of education.

sentences.zh: 明啟帝略，肇定秦都。

translations.literal: He enlightened the emperor's strategy and first settled the Qin capital.
translations.idiomatic: He opened the emperor's design and first secured the Qin heartland.

sentences.zh: 勛成智隱，靜其如愚。

translations.literal: Merit accomplished, wisdom hid, still as though foolish.
translations.idiomatic: Merit won, he hid his wit and seemed almost dull.

sentences.zh: 子翼守溫，蕭公是埒。

translations.literal: Ziyi guarded Wen; he may be paired with Lord Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: Ziyi held Wen; he stands beside Xiao He.

sentences.zh: 系兵轉食，以集鴻烈。

translations.literal: He bound soldiers and turned grain to gather the great enterprise.
translations.idiomatic: He rallied troops and convoyed grain to build the great achievement.

sentences.zh: 誅文屈賈，有剛有折。

translations.literal: He executed Wen and bent Jia—there was hardness, there was yielding.
translations.idiomatic: He slew Wen and broke Jia's pride—now stern, now supple.
